# Building an AI Poet: From Zero to Deployed Web App

**Date:** June 11, 2026  
**Author:** artistjini

---

I built a web application that writes poetry. You type in a topic, and it generates a poem — in real time, in your browser, powered by Google's Gemini AI. It's called **AI Poet**, and it's live.

This post is a record of how I built it: what I learned, what broke, and how I fixed it.

---

## What I Built

**AI Poet** is a Streamlit web app that takes a topic from the user and returns a freshly written poem, generated by Google's Gemini model through the LangChain framework.

**Live app:** [https://artistjini-ai-poet.streamlit.app/](https://artistjini-ai-poet.streamlit.app/)

**Tech stack:**
- Python
- LangChain
- Google Gemini API (`gemini-1.5-flash-latest`)
- Streamlit + Streamlit Community Cloud
- Miniconda (environment management)
- Git + GitHub

---

## What I Learned

### LangChain

LangChain is a framework for building applications on top of large language models. Rather than sending raw prompts directly to an API, LangChain lets you structure the workflow — managing prompts, chaining model calls, and connecting external data sources.

I used it here to keep the code clean and extensible. If I want to swap out the model or add new features later, the architecture makes that straightforward.

### LLM Chain

The core of the app is an LLM Chain: a pipeline that connects three things in sequence.

```
PromptTemplate | LLM | OutputParser
```

The `PromptTemplate` formats the user's topic into a structured instruction for the model. The `LLM` generates the poem. The `OutputParser` extracts the clean text to display. Writing this as a declarative chain — rather than a tangle of imperative calls — made the logic much easier to follow and modify.

### Gemini API

I chose Google's `gemini-1.5-flash-latest` model as the generation engine. It's fast, handles Korean poetry well, and integrates cleanly with LangChain via the `langchain-google-genai` package.

### Streamlit

Streamlit lets you build a complete web UI in pure Python — no HTML, no CSS, no JavaScript. For this project, that was the right tradeoff. I could focus on the AI logic and have a working, deployed interface without learning a frontend framework.

### Miniconda

I managed the project environment with Miniconda. Keeping dependencies isolated per project prevents version conflicts and makes it easy to reproduce the environment elsewhere — including on a cloud deployment server.

---

## How I Built It

### Step 1: Local Development

I set up a dedicated Conda environment and installed the necessary packages: `langchain`, `langchain-google-genai`, and `streamlit`.

In `main.py`, I:
- Loaded the Gemini model using `init_chat_model`
- Wrote a prompt template that takes a topic and instructs the model to write a poem
- Built the Streamlit UI: a title, a text input, a submit button, and a loading spinner

I stored the API key in a local `.env` file and tested everything locally until the poem generation worked end-to-end.

### Step 2: Version Control and GitHub

I initialized a Git repository and made one deliberate change before pushing: I renamed the default branch from `master` to `main`, and updated the global Git config to use `main` as the default for all future projects.

I then used the GitHub CLI (`gh`) to create the remote repository and push the code:

```bash
gh repo create ai-poet --public --source=. --remote=origin --push
```

Remote repo: [https://github.com/artistjini/ai-poet.git](https://github.com/artistjini/ai-poet.git)

### Step 3: Deployment and Debugging

I connected the GitHub repo to Streamlit Community Cloud and triggered the first deployment. It failed. Then I fixed it. Then it failed again, for a different reason. Then I fixed that too.

Here's what went wrong and how I resolved each issue.

---

## Debugging the Deployment

### Issue 1: Dependency Installation Error

**Error:** `Error installing requirements`

**Root cause:** My `requirements.txt` had been generated with absolute local paths embedded in it — paths specific to my machine that the cloud environment couldn't resolve.

**Fix:** I rewrote `requirements.txt` from scratch using the standard format: one package name (and optional version pin) per line. No local paths, no extra metadata. Redeployed. It worked.

### Issue 2: API Authentication Error

**Error:** `ChatGoogleGenerativeAIError` — API key not found

**Root cause:** The cloud environment has no access to my local `.env` file. The API key I'd stored there simply didn't exist from the deployment server's perspective.

**Fix:** Streamlit Community Cloud has a **Secrets** management feature in the dashboard. I added `GOOGLE_API_KEY` there, and updated the app code to read from `st.secrets` instead of a `.env` file. Authentication resolved.

### Issue 3: Quota Exceeded

**Error:** `429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED`

**Root cause:** The free tier of the Gemini API has rate limits. I hit them during testing.

**Fix:** This one didn't require a code change — just reading the logs to confirm the cause, waiting for the quota window to reset, and noting that for any production use, quota management needs to be part of the design upfront.

---

## Outcome

The app is deployed and working. Here's what the final product does:

- Accepts a topic as text input
- Sends it to Gemini via a LangChain chain
- Displays the generated poem in real time
- Includes a reset button to clear the output

More importantly, this project gave me hands-on experience with the full development cycle: local development, version control, cloud deployment, and production debugging. The errors I hit weren't blockers — they were the actual learning.

---
